analysis.summary: A 45-year-old female is experiencing a severe (7/10) full-body rash that has persisted for several months, with no current medications or known allergies.

flags.title: Persistent, Severe Rash
flags.description: A rash lasting for 'months' with a severity of 7/10 indicates a significant, chronic dermatological issue requiring professional medical diagnosis and treatment. Prolonged severe rash can lead to skin breakdown, infection, and significant discomfort.

flags.title: Lack of Contact Information
flags.description: No phone or email provided, which may hinder follow-up communication if needed.

possible_conditions.name: Eczema (Dermatitis)
possible_conditions.explanation: Eczema is a common chronic inflammatory skin condition that can cause persistent, itchy, and severe rashes on various parts of the body.

possible_conditions.name: Psoriasis
possible_conditions.explanation: Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by red, scaly patches on the skin which can be widespread and persist for long durations.

possible_conditions.name: Fungal Infection (e.g., Tinea Corporis/Manuum/Pedis)
possible_conditions.explanation: Widespread or untreated fungal infections can cause persistent, itchy rashes that may last for months, especially if misdiagnosed or inadequately treated.

possible_conditions.name: Contact Dermatitis
possible_conditions.explanation: While less common to be full-body and continuous for months unless there's persistent exposure to an allergen or irritant, it remains a possibility for a chronic rash.

possible_conditions.name: Scabies
possible_conditions.explanation: Scabies is caused by mites and can lead to intensely itchy, widespread rashes that persist for months if not treated, often worsening at night.

possible_conditions.name: Autoimmune Skin Conditions (e.g., Cutaneous Lupus Erythematosus)
possible_conditions.explanation: Some autoimmune diseases can manifest with chronic skin rashes that can be severe and widespread.

recommendations: Schedule an urgent appointment with a dermatologist or primary care provider to properly diagnose the cause of the rash and initiate appropriate treatment.
Avoid scratching the rash as it can worsen irritation and potentially lead to secondary skin infections.
Keep a detailed log of the rash's appearance, severity, any associated symptoms (like itching, pain, fever), and anything that seems to make it better or worse, to share with the doctor.
questions_for_doctor: What is the specific diagnosis for this persistent rash?
What are the recommended treatment options, including topical and systemic medications?
Are there any specific triggers or underlying conditions that might be contributing to the rash?
What measures can be taken to alleviate the severity of the itching and discomfort?
Are any diagnostic tests (e.g., skin biopsy, blood tests) recommended to determine the cause of the rash?
